speaker: interviewee
text: So I started to work in Mikveh Israel. But I wanted badly to study. So all-- I went to Jerusalem, and somehow I went to the Department of Psychology. I could have done something else, but that's what happened. And I started a thing that I never did before. I never had regular, formal studies. And because of this, the first two exams I failed. I failed four times in my life in exams, two driver licenses and [LAUGHS] these two. But I somehow organized myself, and I became a good student. And I finished in three years, as old this program was. But one of the exams, in middle of the exams, I was called that my grandfather passed away. And I left the exam, and I went to the funeral. And therefore, I didn't get my-- my BA until a year later. But that's not terrible. And I somehow found a way to get on a direct doctorate-- direct track for PhD at the Sorbonne in Paris. French I knew quite well. So I became again a delegate of the Jewish Agency, again in the [INAUDIBLE] with the authorization from their part also to study. And it took me two years. And I finished all the exams of my PhD, except for the thesis. For the dissertation. But three days after, after I presented the last finals, I got a call from my brother-in-law from Israel that our son-- Aharon was his name, and the name of my wife's father-- Aharon, who was in the yeshiva of Nehalim-- that he fell sick, and that I should come over. I took the same day the plane. And I found him in the hospital. And I found him coming out from a [? councilium ?] of about seven doctors, who established the diagnosis he had-- that he had a tumor in the brain. He was done-- operated, and we were hopeful that he will survive. And he had a very nice summer. He went back to the yeshiva and had good grades and studied well. But at the end of the vacation, of the summer vacation, he-- the-- deterioration started. He was brought again to the hospital. They made a second operation, but they just didn't do anything any more. And he passed away as a child of close to 15 years old. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [BACKROUND NOISE] After suffering quite a lot. A wonderful child that we still miss until today. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And because of this tragic event in our family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stopped my studies. I didn't do anything with the dissertation. I couldn't get myself to do it. I was fortunate to have Bilha, my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is B-I-L-H-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We married in 1952, in Israel. She supported me all the time. And I wouldn't have studied without her support. But in those times, we needed so badly each other. So we strengthened one another, [SIGHS] and somehow we tried to keep our head above the waters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the names of your other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I want something to mention before. A few months before that, my mother passed away. And I forgot to arrange for someone to say a few words at her grave. So I had to do it by myself. And I remember saying to my mother in that time, despite the fact that I knew exactly what's going to happen to our son-- so I said to her, I want you to know, Mother, that we are going to continue to be anchored in life. We are going to bring home girls for our sons, and boys for our girls, and we shall build and continue to have a happy, living family.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, it was very difficult to say. It was much more difficult to do. But we did that, too. What was your question, please? The names of your other children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So in addition to the late Aharon, [HEBREW]-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we have to long life five children. Assenath, A-- double S-- E-N-A-T-H, who is married to David Lichtenstein, L-I-C-H-T-E-N-S-T-E-I-N. And they have five children. They are living in Kidumim, in the freed territories, the liberated territories. Then we have another daughter called Chaya Nehama. She's married to Dr. [? Dov ?] Hornick, H-O-R-N-I-C-K. And they have four children. And then I have our son, [? Shuli ?] Shalom Yosef, or Shalom Yosef [? Shuli, ?] who is a famous actor in Israel. Very, very special person. He is married to [? Michal ?] [? Danino, ?] and they have two children. And then [? Efrat, ?] who is married to [? Yeoshua ?] Tessler, T-E-S-S-L-E-R. And they have three children. And then we have Yehudit, who is married to Abraham Eisenfeld, E-I-S-E-N-F-E-L-D, which is a special marriage. Yehudit is a Down syndrome person, and Abraham has motor impairments. And they found each other. They love and cherish each other. They have a beautiful apartment, and they live together, happy. And they're expecting us to come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you now live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, [SIGHS] I have still to add something. In 1971, I broke a bone. I fell and I broke a bone. And because of this, I was about six months-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't do whatever I wanted. I was months in the-- weeks in the hospital. I had been operated, and so on, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that period, I finished my PhD. And in December 1971, I became Dr. Yaacov Rand. What was the question I-- Where do you now live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live now in the Bnei-Brak. After we came back from Paris, we had an apartment in Bnei-Brak, B-N-E-I dash B-R-A-K. And in the actual apartment, we live since 1969.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work do you now do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The assumption is that I do something. Good. I'm a professor in special education. I am a psychologist by profession. And I teach also a lot of subjects in psychology. I am a professor at the Bar-Ilan-- at the School of Education of the Bar-Ilan University in Israel. And actually, a visiting professor at Touro College, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spend your free time?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here? I spend the free time, first of all, by seeing a lot of things in New York. Both my wife Bilha and I, we love cultural things. We are both musical. We go to concerts. We go to rehearsals of concerts. We go to theater. We go to museums. We read a lot. And we have friends. And since when we went to Israel, a lot of our friends went immediately to the travel agents to purchase tickets.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your religious affiliations now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that I am-- first of all, I'm Orthodox. I'm modern. I'm a kind of a combination. I'm open-minded. I'm very much Zionist. I am saying that my Zionism is religious. I'm not fanatic at all. I do not care too much how other people behave in terms of religion. And I can be a good friend with any human being, be he or she Jewish or not Jewish, or religious or not religious. But even with the ultra-Orthodox, I am good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever question the existence of HaShem?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want a yes or no answer, the answer is yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately after the Holocaust. And even more so during my army service. Why more so? Because during-- immediately after the Holocaust, I was so involved in doing that I didn't have time to ask myself philosophical questions. But in the army, I had a lot of leisure time. And there I asked myself questions like this. It was perhaps less the question about the existence of HaShem as the question of me being religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: What resolved that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What resolved that? I think the basic thing was a very strong identification with my father. And then, my-- I do not want to say too much, but my knowledge in Judaics-- in Judaic materials. I was very anchored in Judaism.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm not kind of an outsider of Judaism. So it was me. So from all the alternatives, that was the best one for me. And I think that to me, being religious is also part of my struggle for survival. It has kept me during the Holocaust, and it's a part of me, of my moral survival, until nowadays. Do you have any dreams or nightmares as a result of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have nightmares. I didn't have nightmares, even immediately after the Holocaust. Some people say that I have very powerful repression capacities. I don't know. I never tested that. I'm not sure it is so or not. But I really didn't have nightmares, despite the fact that I saw a lot of atrocities. I saw a lot of terrible pictures. I lived with death for years. But I didn't have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nightmares.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dreams? As everybody, I have very nice dreams. I have dreams that I would like to see all my family, my children, my grandchildren, and their offsprings later on, to be happy and good human beings, and to be loyal to themselves, to their people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my dream is still to see peace in Israel around us. When I say peace in Israel, I mean within Israel and with our neighbors. And we should stop living in this chaotic world that we are in, and come back a little bit to the times of the Kaiser Franz Joseph, where-- [LAUGHS] the-- in the Austro-Hungarian empire, where things were quiet. Not so hectic like they are. We live a killing life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your Sefer Torah now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sefer Torah is now in Israel, in my synagogue, [INAUDIBLE], dominion of the youngsters. So you see, I'm one of those youngsters. Maybe, because of my spirit, I may still call myself as a youngster. It is there. And I have a document from the synagogue that that's my property. I wouldn't give it away to anyone. And if you want to know, I even-- in my will, I wrote down who should-- what will happen with this special Sefer Torah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you now like to deliver to the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: What, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what message would you now like to deliver to the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are a number of things. First of all, that human beings have stop to hate each other. There is no reason with that-- for that. [BACKROUND NOISE] In the Talmud, there is a Mishnah which explains why human beings were created one man alone. And it gives there four reasons. One reason is in order to emphasize the importance of each human individual. That who makes live a person is like making live an entire world. And to destroy a person is like destroying an entire live-- world. And the second reason, that nobody could say there are many gods in heaven. One-- there is a direct relation between one god and one human being. And the third reason, that nobody could say to the other's eye, my father was more important than yours. And the fourth reason is to tell the greatness of God. That when a king in flesh and blood stamps coins with the same stamps-- with the same stamp, they are all alike to each other. But God stamped all of us in his own image, and we are all different from each other. Now, you take these four principles-- the believe in God, the belief that every individual is important, the belief that no one has the right to be superior to someone else, and the belief that we have all the right to be different from each other-- when you combine this, I think it's a wonderful message to humanity. And to this, I would like to add a saying. There is a Hasidic, Kabbalistic legend saying that in heaven, there is an angel who-- whose duty is to fly in this world, into the dark spots of this world, and look for the little sparks of light. To collect them, to pick them up and collect them. And to put them together in a big torch to illuminate the entire world. I think that we all have to do a little bit the work of this angel. We have to look in everybody to see those little sparks of light. Don't concentrate on the shades. Look for the light. And then, don't leave it isolated. Pick it up, bring it together with the light of others. You are going, ultimately, to have this wonderful torch to illuminate the entire world. So that's a message that I can say at this occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you on behalf of the Shoah Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to add one thing. I want to thank you. And I'm happy that I had the opportunity to say what I said. There were many, many years that I couldn't tell to anybody anything. It took me a lot of time until I started. And now, I really think that these things should not get lost. Thank you, both of you. It was a pleasure working with you. This picture, this is my wife Bilha and myself in 1965, touring in Switzerland as a young couple, at a cascade which is the [INAUDIBLE] in Switzerland. This picture has been taken in Carlsbad, Czechoslovakia, at a tour we made together, my brother and sister-in-law. At the left of the picture, Professor Reuven Feuerstein, who is also the laureate of the Israeli [INAUDIBLE] Israel. His wife [? Bertha, ?] myself, and Bilha, my wife. This picture has been taken in Mitzpe Hoshaya, at the house of our daughter Chaya. And it was sent to us while being here in New York so that we can have everybody on the picture. There are still two persons of our family missing here. This is our daughter Yehudit and her husband Abraham, that for one or another reason, they-- they didn't come at this-- to this occasion. Going from left to the right, you have here the baby, called [? Ophir. ?] And then you have our daughter [? Efrat. ?] Next to her, you have another daughter, called Chaya. And she keeps in her hands her son, [? Ephi. ?] Then you have her son, Yonadov. Next to him, there's the son of [? Efrat, ?] called David. And then you have the son of Assenath-- of Assenath, called Uriel. And next to him, there is the son of our son [? Shuli, ?] called Daniel. And then you have the husband of [? Efrat, ?] [? Yeoshua. ?] And then you have the daughter of Chaya, Hadassah-- Hadas, sorry. And then you have next to her, her sister Merav. And then you have the father of the family, Chaya's husband, Dr. [? Dovi ?] Hornick. Then you have the twins, Rahel and Livnat. And then you have a baby, called David [INAUDIBLE], who is the son of our-- [? of Shuli. ?] And then you have our daughter Assenath, her little son Yehuda. Next to her, her daughter [? Noa, ?] her husband David. And on the floor, my son-- our son [? Shuli. ?] But at the right, extreme corner, there is one uncle that's the eldest brother of my wife, called [? Yasha. ?] He was in Russia, succeeded to come over Romania, and from there, to make his aliyah, living now in Israel. This picture has been taken in the house of our brother-in-law [? Shimshon, ?] and [? Nehama, ?] in Nehalim, in Israel. From left to the right, our daughter Yehudit, the-- the-- the wife of our son, our-- our daughter-in-law [? Michal, ?] her husband [? Shuli. ?] Next to him, Professor Reuven Feuerstein. And at the right corner, our daughter Assenath. The occasion was the Sheva Brachot of Shuli and Michal. This is a picture from the newspaper, And you see there our son [? Shuli, ?] who is a famous cinema and theater actor in Israel, working at a theater [INAUDIBLE] at the occasion of getting the award of the best actor of the year, after he has got already the-- what is called [INAUDIBLE], the Oscar-- the Israeli Oscar for film, for cinema. And look what a nice yarmulke he wears at this occasion. This picture is taken outdoors, at night, in Mitzpe Hoshaya, at the occasion of the Sheva Brachot of the couple that you see in the center, which is our daughter Yehudit, and her husband Abraham Eisenfeld. All the others are guests not belonging to our family. This picture is taken in Tel Aviv, at the occasion of the wedding of our daughter Yehudit. As you can see, she is the bride sitting in the bride chair. And she is surrounded by three of the bridesmaids. They are all-- the three are cousins of her-- nieces of her. And that is at the left side [? Noa, ?] in the middle Meirav, and at the right side Hadas. This picture is, again, taken at the occasion of the Sheva Brachot in Mitzpe Hoshaya. You have at the left side our daughter Efrat, and at the right side [HEBREW], Yehudit. I want to say at this occasion that this wedding, and all afterwards, was such an experience that it is considered by us as one of the most important events of our life. In this picture, you see four of our daughters and two grandchildren. At the left, it is [? Ephi, ?] the son of Chaya. And in the hands of Assenath is the-- the-- is David-- I'm sorry, is [? Ofrit. ?] Yehudit is standing, and at the right is Chaya and [? Efrat. ?] This is our eldest grandchild, Rachel. She is now about 14 and a half to 15 years, and she lives in Kedumim, a very good student, and likes to play music. This is her younger sister, younger with five minutes, a twin sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Livnath. And she's also a brilliant student, and also likes very much music. They play piano and flute. These are the two eldest boy grandchildren. They are just good friends. At the left side is Yonadov. At the right side is Uriel. They are very talented. Yonadov plays the clarinet, and Uriel the saxophone. These are three bandits. Little bandits. [LAUGHS] They are all living in Mitzpe Hoshaya. They are good friends. From time to time they struggle with each other. At the left side is Eiran, in the mid is [? Ephi ?] and at the right side is David. It's very difficult for me to tell you which one is more cute than the other. This is Assenath's youngest son. His name is Yehuda, after his grandfather, Yehuda Lichtenstein, the father of David. And he is just terrific. First of all, I would like to say that I'm very sorry that we don't have here in New York good pictures from all of our grandchildren. So some of them are not-- were not mentioned. They were mentioned only in the general picture, like Daniel and others. But I hope there will be some other occasion that I'll be able to make videos of them. The last picture is myself, 1996, in Touro College-- can you-- how do you call it here-- the commencement ceremonies, dressed as a professor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are in our little apartment in [INAUDIBLE] Park, my wife Bilha and myself. She is my loyal companion of about 50 years. And we had a very nice and hectic, happy life, despite all the problems that we had in our life. She was always very supporting to me, and in all my endeavors. And I tried to do-- do the same to her. I'm not so sure of the success, but I tried, at least. And I am happy that we can, all of us, sit here at our age and tell stories from our past. Thank you both.
